attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

message.content: This project is still back-dating time events. When my computer wakes up in the morning it asks me what I was up to. I select "break", but it back-dates that event to when the computer was last turned off. Right? That's not great

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- git-deploy: Guides Qamcom team members through git workflows on the self-hosted GitLab at gitlab.qamcom.se. Use this skill whenever the user wants to set up git, clone a repo, push changes, create a branch, deploy to the staging environment, or create a merge request on the Qamcom GitLab. Also trigger when the user asks how to get started with the repo, how to deploy their changes, how to push to GitLab, or how to open an MR. Covers Linux, macOS, and Windows.
- migrate-to-tng: Migrate an existing Qamcom webapp project to the new TNG portal/registry platform. Use this skill whenever the user asks to migrate a project, says "migrate to TNG", references the qamcom-apps-template, or asks to set up CI/CD for the new portal. Also trigger when the user mentions setting up manifest.json, the announcer, kubeseal secrets, or postgres migration for a Qamcom project.
- restore-prod-backup: Restores a named MongoDB database from the latest prod backup onto the local machine. Use this skill whenever the user says anything like "restore [database name]", "restore the latest [name]", "pull down [db] from prod", or "reset [db] to prod data". Always requires a database name — never proceed without one.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- caveman:caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- caveman:caveman-commit: Ultra-compressed commit message generator. Cuts noise from commit messages while preserving intent and reasoning. Conventional Commits format. Subject ≤50 chars, body only when "why" isn't obvious. Use when user says "write a commit", "commit message", "generate commit", "/commit", or invokes /caveman-commit. Auto-triggers when staging changes.
- caveman:caveman-help: Quick-reference card for all caveman modes, skills, and commands. One-shot display, not a persistent mode. Trigger: /caveman-help, "caveman help", "what caveman commands", "how do I use caveman".
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:compress: Compress natural language memory files (CLAUDE.md, todos, preferences) into caveman format to save input tokens. Preserves all technical substance, code, URLs, and structure. Compressed version overwrites the original file. Human-readable backup saved as FILE.original.md. Trigger: /caveman:compress <filepath> or "compress memory file"
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

<result>Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile my findings.

## Summary: Wake-up Detection and Time Event Creation

Based on a thorough search of the codebase, here are the relevant locations for handling computer wake-up detection and time event creation:

### 1. **Wake/Sleep Detection Setup**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift`

**Lines 115-127** - Screen monitoring setup:
- Registers for `NSWorkspace.screensDidWakeNotification` (when screen wakes)
- Registers for `NSWorkspace.screensDidSleepNotification` (when screen sleeps)
- Also monitors screensaver start/stop events via `DistributedNotificationCenter`

### 2. **Wake Detection Handler**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift`

**Lines 129-139** - `@objc func screenWoke()`:
- Triggered when screen/display wakes from sleep
- Records screen-on event
- Checks if enough time has passed since last prompt (`sinceLastPrompt > promptInterval`)
- If threshold met and user not logged out: stores the sleep time in `screenSleepTime` as `pendingPromptEntryTime` and triggers `askForProject(isAutoPrompt: true)` to prompt user

### 3. **Sleep Detection Handler**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift`

**Lines 140-148** - `@objc func screenSlept()`:
- Triggered when screen/display goes to sleep
- Captures the time in `screenSleepTime` (used later as the departure timestamp)
- Records the current project entry at sleep time

### 4. **User Prompt for Activity**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/ProjectPromptWindow.swift`

**Lines 3-192** - ProjectPromptWindow class:
- Shows UI with "Working on?" text field (line 55)
- User can input project name, click Save, Break, or Skip (lines 75-99)
- Window displays "What's up?" when idle (line 106)
- Two view states: idle state and input state (lines 101-147)

### 5. **Timestamp Capture and Event Creation**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift`

**Lines 507-551** - `func askForProject(isAutoPrompt: Bool)`:
- **Line 527:** Captures the return time: `let returnTime = Date()` (when user returns and prompt is shown)
- **Lines 529-533:** When user saves via `onSave` callback, records project entry at the captured return time with comment "project starts at return, not departure"
- **Lines 535-539:** When user clicks "Break", records Break entry at return time
- **Lines 541-546:** Cleanup via `onDismiss` callback

### 6. **Project Entry Recording**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift`

**Lines 241-247** - `func recordProjectEntry(_ project: String, at time: Date = Date())`:
- Appends ProjectEntry with specified timestamp to event data
- Also logs to CSV event log via `eventLog.append(at: time, ...)`
- Schedules next prompt timer

### 7. **Event Log Timestamp**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTrackerCore/EventLog.swift`

**Lines 26-33** - `func append(at date: Date = Date(), ...)`:
- Records event with date/time split into date (yyyy-MM-dd) and time (HH:mm:ss) components
- Appends to CSV file with columns: date, time, event_type, event_source, project_name

### 8. **Screensaver Wake-up Variant**
**File:** `/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift`

**Lines 155-169** - `@objc func screensaverStopped()`:
- Alternative wake path when screensaver ends
- Sets `pendingPromptEntryTime = start` (line 162) to capture screensaver start time
- Delays 0.5 seconds then calls `askForProject(isAutoPrompt: true)` (lines 164-166)
- Triggers only if absence > 60 seconds

### Key Data Structures
- **ProjectEntry** (`/Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTrackerCore/TimeCalculations.swift`, lines 19-27): Contains `project: String` and `time: Date`
- **ScreenEvent** (lines 5-17): Contains `kind` and `time` for tracking screen on/off/screensaver states

The overall flow: Display wakes → `screenWoke()` triggered → checks time since last prompt → `askForProject()` shows UI → captures `returnTime` when window created → user responds → `recordProjectEntry()` called with that timestamp → event logged to CSV with split date/time fields.</result>

133	    private func activate() {
134	        idleContainer.isHidden = true
135	        inputContainer.isHidden = false
136	        makeKey()

139	
140	    func show(startActive: Bool = false) {
141	        if startActive {
142	            idleContainer.isHidden = true
143	            inputContainer.isHidden = false
144	        } else {
145	            idleContainer.isHidden = false
146	            inputContainer.isHidden = true
147	        }
148	
149	        // Recalculate position each time — screen layout may have changed (e.g. after screensaver)
150	        let w = ProjectPromptWindow.W
151	        let h = ProjectPromptWindow.H
152	        let screen = NSScreen.main ?? NSScreen.screens[0]
153	        let menuBarHeight = screen.frame.height - screen.visibleFrame.maxY
154	        let x = screen.frame.midX - w / 2
155	        let y = screen.frame.maxY - menuBarHeight - h - 10
156	        setFrameOrigin(NSPoint(x: x, y: y))
157	
158	        orderFrontRegardless()
159	        if startActive {
160	            makeKey()
161	            makeFirstResponder(comboBox)
162	        }
163	    }
164	
165	    override func performKeyEquivalent(with event: NSEvent) -> Bool {
166	        if event.modifierFlags.intersection(.deviceIndependentFlagsMask) == .command,
167	           event.charactersIgnoringModifiers == "a" {
168	            return NSApp.sendAction(#selector(NSText.selectAll(_:)), to: nil, from: self)
169	        }
170	        return super.performKeyEquivalent(with: event)
171	    }
172	
173	    @objc private func saveAction() {
174	        let val = comboBox.stringValue.trimmingCharacters(in: .whitespaces)
175	        if !val.isEmpty { onSave?(val) }
176	        dismiss()
177	    }
178	
179	    @objc private func breakAction() {
180	        onBreak?()
181	        dismiss()
182	    }
183	
184	    @objc private func skipAction() {
185	        dismiss()
186	    }
187	
188	    private func dismiss() {
189	        close()
190	        onDismiss?()
191	    }
192	}

197	    override func acceptsFirstMouse(for event: NSEvent?) -> Bool { true }
198	}

input.description: Show recent events from CSV log

content.text:
Found the bug. Look at this from the log:

```
2026-06-18,08:12:41,on,computer,-   ← screen woke today
2026-06-17,18:19:58,entry,user,Break ← Break recorded at 6:19pm YESTERDAY
2026-06-18,08:31:20,entry,user,2550 spark
```

Break was created today (it appears after the 8:12am on-event in the log) but stored with yesterday's 6:19pm timestamp. That's the stale `returnTime` from an old prompt window.

The flow:
1. Timer fires at ~6:19pm yesterday → `askForProject` → `returnTime = Date()` (6:19pm) → window shown
2. User never dismisses it → computer sleeps with window still open
3. Morning: `screenWoke()` fires → `promptWindow != nil` → guard exits early, reschedules timer
4. Window still open, user clicks Break → closure uses old captured `returnTime` (6:19pm yesterday)

**Fix:** on wake, close the stale prompt window so a fresh one opens with correct `returnTime`.

The `promptWindow` guard inside `askForProject` is the problem. I'll fix it by closing a stale prompt on wake before asking again — rather than letting the old window with its captured `returnTime` linger.

lines: // Prompt on lid-open return if absent long enough (screensaver path handles its own)
+            // Discard any existing prompt — its captured returnTime is stale (pre-sleep).

content.content: The file /Users/srdkvr/source/BarTimeTracker/Sources/BarTimeTracker/AppDelegate.swift has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: // Delay slightly — screen layout isn't stable the instant the screensaver ends
+                    // Discard any stale prompt window — its returnTime predates the screensaver.

content.text: Root cause: prompt window opened at 6:19pm, computer slept with it still open, user clicked Break next morning — closure had `returnTime` from 6:19pm captured. Fix closes the stale window on both wake paths before opening a fresh one with the correct timestamp.

content: Fixed a bug where the Break entry was timestamped from the previous evening instead of wake time. No next action needed — the fix is in place. (disable recaps in /config)
